content:
5 min read Just now -- A small hack to make your big story easy to read Medium is a fantastic platform for sharing ideas, but one thing it lacks is a native Table of Contents feature. Long-form articles — especially those with many sections, equations, and figures — can feel overwhelming without a clear navigation structure. Readers often scroll endlessly, looking for a specific topic or formula. In this article, I’ll show you a simple and effective hack to build a Table of Contents for your Medium stories using Python and BeautifulSoup. This approach works even for draft articles, and it will save you hours of manual linking. Table of Contents Topics How to build a table of contents for your article in Medium Why a Table of Contents? The Hack: How It Works The Python Solution Step 1: Save Your Article as HTML Step 2: Run the Python Script Step 3: Copy and Paste into Medium Figures Figure 1. Random Figure Figure 2. Editing the Table of Contents Why a Table of Contents? A Table of Contents (TOC) improves the reader experience by: • Providing an overview of the article’s structure. • Allowing quick navigation to specific sections. • Reducing friction for readers who want to jump to a particular topic. • Making long-form content more digestible and professional. Medium does not automatically generate TOCs, but you can create one manually using anchor links. The trick is to use the unique IDs that Medium assigns to each heading, equation, and figure. The Hack: How It Works Every heading, image, and embed in Medium has a unique HTML ID. These IDs are generated automatically when you publish or save a draft. They look like #91d6 or #heading-1. By linking to these IDs, you can create a TOC that scrolls the reader directly to the corresponding section. The challenge is finding these IDs — Medium does not display them in the editor. This is where our Python script comes in. The Python Solution We’ll use BeautifulSoup to parse the HTML of your Medium article (saved as a .html file) and extract all the elements that matter: headings, equations, tables, and figures. The script then generates a formatted TOC with the correct anchor links. Get Guilherme Ziegler’s stories in your inbox Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer. Remember me for faster sign in First of all let’s add some random picture here and try to add it inside the table of content, Figure 1. Press enter or click to view image in full size Figure 1. Random FigureNow lets follow the next steps to get it in the previous table of content section we definied earlier. Step 1: Save Your Article as HTML • Open your Medium draft or published article in a browser. • Right-click and select “Save As…” (or press Ctrl + S). • Choose “Web Page, HTML Only” and save the file. I did while writting and got a html file named: html_file = 'How Banks Should Lend Their Money _ by Guilherme Ziegler _ Medium'Step 2: Run the Python Script Here’s the complete script: from bs4 import BeautifulSoup import redef extract_elements(html_path): """ Extracts all elements with IDs from a Medium HTML file: equations, tables, figures, and headings. Returns a dictionary with categorized elements. """ with open(html_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f: html = f.read() soup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'html.parser') elements = {'equations': [], 'tables': [], 'figures': [], 'headings': []} # Extract equations, tables, and figures for figure in soup.find_all('figure'): figcaption = figure.find('figcaption') if not figcaption: continue title = figcaption.get_text(strip=True) # Find the ID from the previous sibling (usually a

tag) prev = figure.find_previous_sibling() element_id = prev.get('id') if prev and prev.get('id') else None # If no ID found, try the next

tag if not element_id: next_p = figure.find_next('p') if next_p and next_p.get('id'): element_id = next_p.get('id') if not element_id: continue if 'Equation' in title or 'Eq.' in title: elements['equations'].append({'title': title, 'id': element_id}) elif 'Table' in title or 'Tab.' in title: elements['tables'].append({'title': title, 'id': element_id}) elif 'Figure' in title or 'Fig.' in title: elements['figures'].append({'title': title, 'id': element_id}) # Extract headings (H1, H2, H3) for tag in ['h1', 'h2', 'h3']: for element in soup.find_all(tag): text = element.get_text(strip=True) if text and element.get('id'): elements['headings'].append({ 'title': text, 'id': element.get('id') }) # Add any important H4 headings manually if needed # Example: elements['headings'].append({'title': 'Price (French Amortization) System', 'id': 'e29f'}) return elements def generate_toc(elements): """ Generates a formatted Table of Contents from the extracted elements. """ print("## Table of Contents\n") print("### Topics\n") for h in elements['headings']: print(f"- {h['title']} (#{h['id']})") print("\n### Equations\n") for eq in elements['equations']: print(f"- {eq['title']} (#{eq['id']})") print("\n### Tables\n") for tb in elements['tables']: print(f"- {tb['title']} (#{tb['id']})") print("\n### Figures\n") for fig in elements['figures']: print(f"- {fig['title']} (#{fig['id']})") def save_toc_to_file(elements, output_path="toc_output.txt"): """ Saves the Table of Contents to a text file. """ with open(output_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f: f.write("## Table of Contents\n\n") f.write("### Topics\n") for h in elements['headings']: f.write(f"- {h['title']} (#{h['id']})\n") f.write("\n### Equations\n") for eq in elements['equations']: f.write(f"- {eq['title']} (#{eq['id']})\n") f.write("\n### Tables\n") for tb in elements['tables']: f.write(f"- {tb['title']} (#{tb['id']})\n") f.write("\n### Figures\n") for fig in elements['figures']: f.write(f"- {fig['title']} (#{fig['id']})\n") print(f"✅ TOC saved to {output_path}") The first function, extract_elements, does the heavy lifting. It opens the HTML file you saved from your Medium article, scans through the code, and picks out every element that has an ID — headings, equations, tables, and figures. For each one, it stores both the title and the corresponding ID, so we know exactly where to link later. The second function, generate_toc, takes that information and turns it into a clean, formatted Table of Contents. It prints the TOC directly to your screen, organized by categories, with each item displayed as a clickable-looking link. You can simply copy the output and paste it straight into your Medium article — no extra formatting needed. The third function, save_toc_to_file, does the same thing but saves the TOC to a text file instead. This is useful if you want to edit the list before pasting it, or if you'd like to keep a backup for future reference. Step 3: Copy and Paste into Medium The output will look like this: ## Table of Contents### Topics - How to build a table of contents for your article in Medium (#6927) - Table of Contents (#963f) - Why a Table of Contents? (#05d4) - The Hack: How It Works (#4dcb) - The Python Solution (#4abc) - Step 1: Save Your Article as HTML (#4834) - Step 2: Run the Python Script (#02ad) - Step 3: Copy and Paste into Medium (#00e6) - Final Thoughs (#b2b4) ### Equations ### Tables ### Figures - Figure 1. Random Figure (#021f) - Figure 2. Editing the Table of Contents (#4971) ✅ TOC saved to toc_output.txt Bingo, Tada, Et voilà, Abracadabra! Now, all you have to do is copy this into your Table of Contents and replace the number inside the brackets with the actual link — as shown in Figure 2. Figure 2. Editing the Table of ContentsFinal Thoughs Well, that was quick! Not my usual writing style, but I thought it could improve the writing experience for some of you who write like I do. Happy writing! 🚀 P.S. If you’d like to see the full Python code in action, check out my article “How Banks Should Lend Their Money”, where I used this exact method to build a complete Table of Contents for over 25 equations, 8 tables, and 2 figures.
